Expresses its deep concern at the continuing reports of serious human rights violations and abuses committed by the military and security forces, as well as violations of international humanitarian law committed in Myanmar against Rohingya and other minorities, in particular in the southern Chin State and in the Kachin, Rakhine and Shan States, including arbitrary arrests, death in detention, torture and other cruel, inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment, murder and intentional attacks on the physical integrity of children, recruitment and use of children for forced labour, indiscriminate shelling of civilian areas, destruction, including by fire, housing, deprivation of economic and social rights, forced displacement of more than 860,000 Rohingya and other minorities in Bangladesh, rape, sexual slavery and other forms of gender-based sexual violence, as well as restrictions on the exercise of the right to freedom of religion or belief, expression and peaceful assembly, restrictions on freedom of the media and other restrictions on or access to the Internet;

2. Strongly condemns all human rights violations and abuses in Myanmar, and stresses the importance of independent, fair and transparent international investigations into gross human rights violations in Myanmar, including sexual and gender-based violence and violations and abuses against women and children, and of holding accountable those responsible for brutal acts and crimes against all persons, including the Rohingya, in order to provide justice to victims through all national, regional and international legal instruments and judicial mechanisms, including the International Court of Justice and the International Criminal Court, as appropriate;

3. Urges the army and security forces of Myanmar and other armed groups, in particular in the Rakhine, Chin and Shan States, to hear the Secretary-General's calls for a global ceasefire, to end all hostilities and to resolve grievances through political dialogue;

4. Takes note of the order of 23 January 2020 of the International Court of Justice indicating interim measures, and urges the Government of Myanmar to comply with the provisions of the order in so far as they concern the Rohingya in its territory and to do everything in its power to prevent the commission of all acts referred to in article 2 of the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide, to ensure that none of these acts, inter alia, are committed by its military units, by non-conventional armed units that may be under its authority or with its support, or by organizations or persons that may be placed under its control, authority or influence, to prevent the destruction of evidence and to ensure its preservation, and to report to the Court on all measures taken to give effect to the order;

5. Expresses grave concern at the restrictions on humanitarian access in all areas of conflict, in particular in the Rakhine and Chin States, as well as at the limited scope of measures taken to ensure access to health care for Rohingya, in particular during the period of VOCID-19, and urges the Government of Myanmar to demonstrate full cooperation and full, unimpeded and unsupervised access to all United Nations human rights mandate-holders and mechanisms, including the Special Rapporteur on the situation of human rights in Myanmar, the United Nations Special Monitoring and Information Task Force on Serious Violations against Children, the Independent Inquiry Mechanism for Myanmar established by the Human Rights Council in its resolution 39/2, relevant United Nations agencies and regional and international human rights bodies, to enable them to monitor independently the situation of human rights and to ensure that persons can cooperate with such mechanisms without hindrance and without fear of reprisal, intimidation or attacks by the international community, and declares that the international community remains seriously concerned by the international community, including the United Nations in all areas, and that the international community remains deeply concerned by the fact that the international community remains concerned by the international community in all areas, including the United Nations, including the United Nations, including the United Nations, including the affected

6. Urges the United Nations to ensure that the Independent Inquiry Mechanism for Myanmar established by the Human Rights Council in its resolution 39/2 enjoys the flexibility it needs in terms of staffing, premises and operational freedom, in order to fulfil its mandate to the best of its ability, and urges Myanmar, Member States, the judiciary and private entities to cooperate fully with the Mechanism, including by providing access and assistance in the discharge of its mandate;

7. Expresses grave concern at the risk of further trauma to victims of human rights violations and violations, in particular children and survivors of sexual violence, and urges all actors engaged in evidence-gathering activities to follow the principle of "do not harm" in order to respect the dignity of victims and avoid further trauma, and calls upon Myanmar to take fully into account the needs of victims and survivors and the right of such persons to an effective remedy,

items_en: 8. Reiterates its call upon the Government of Myanmar to take the following urgent measures:

(a) To put an immediate end to all violence and violations of international law committed in Myanmar, to ensure the protection of the human rights of all persons living in Myanmar, including Rohingya and members of other minorities, and to take all necessary measures to ensure justice for the victims, to ensure the establishment of all responsibilities and to end impunity for all violations of and violations of human rights law and international humanitarian law, beginning with a full, transparent and independent investigation of all such violations, and requests the President of Myanmar to publish in full the report of the independent commission of inquiry established by the Government of Myanmar in 2018 or to transmit its findings to the relevant international mechanisms;
(b) To demonstrate a clear political will, supported by concrete actions, for the sustainable and voluntary return of Rohingya Muslims in safety and dignity;

(c) To create the necessary conditions for the sustainable and voluntary return, in their places of origin, in dignity and security, of all refugees, including Rohingya Muslim refugees, bearing in mind, inter alia, that Rohingya has so far not been returned under the bilateral repatriation mechanism established by Bangladesh and Myanmar, owing to the inability of the Government of Myanmar to create such conditions in the Rakhine State;

(d) To establish confidence-building measures among Rohingya Muslims in the camps in Bangladesh, including through direct communication between Rohingya representatives and the Myanmar authorities and through the organization of reconnaissance visits to the Rakhine State by Rohingya representatives;

(e) To ensure the full protection of the human rights and fundamental freedoms of all in Myanmar, including for Rohingya Muslims and other minorities, with equality and dignity, without discrimination, to prevent further instability and insecurity, alleviate suffering, address the root causes of the crisis, including by repealing or reforming discriminatory laws, and find a viable and lasting solution;

(f) To fulfil its human rights obligations and its commitments to protect the right to freedom of expression, including online, and the right to freedom of association and peaceful assembly, and to create and maintain conditions for civil society and the independent media to act safely and peacefully;

(g) Take the necessary measures to prevent the spread of discrimination and prejudice and to combat incitement to hatred against Rohingya Muslims and members of other minorities, publicly condemn such acts and oppose hate speech while fully respecting international human rights law, promote interfaith dialogue in cooperation with the international community and encourage political and religious leaders in the country to work towards community reconciliation and national unity through dialogue, and implement the Peacebuilding Fund project to combat hate speech;

(h) Ensure an inclusive response to VOCID-19 to protect all individuals and communities in accordance with international humanitarian and human rights law, including Rohingya Muslims and members of other minorities;

(i) Accelerate measures to eliminate statelessness and systematic and institutionalized discrimination against members of ethnic and religious minorities, in particular Rohingya Muslims, including by reviewing and reforming the 1982 Citizenship Act, which has resulted in a denial of human rights, ensuring equal access to full citizenship, through a transparent, voluntary and inclusive procedure and all civil and political rights, allowing self-identification, through the amendment or repeal of all discriminatory laws and policies, including discriminatory provisions in the 2015 series of "Laws on the Protection of Race and Religion" on religious conversions, interfaith marriages, monogamy and control of population growth, and repealing all local decrees that restrict the right to freedom of movement and access to registration of civil status, health and education services and livelihoods;

(j) Dismantling camps for internally displaced persons in the Rakhine State without further delay, within a specified time frame, by ensuring that the return and resettlement of internally displaced persons is carried out in accordance with international standards and best practices, in cooperation with the United Nations and the international community, such as the Guiding Principles on Internal Displacement; 15
(k) Accelerate the full implementation of all recommendations of the Consultative Commission on the Rakhine State to address the root causes of the crisis;

(l) Establish a permanent system for the holding of free, fair, credible, transparent and inclusive general elections, allowing for the unhindered participation of all, regardless of their ethnic or religious identity or status, including Rohingya Muslims and members of other minorities;

(m) To continue to work with the United Nations to implement the remaining parties to the joint plan of action to halt and prevent the recruitment and use of children by all armed forces, including Tatmadaw, and to fill protection gaps by undertaking with the monitoring and reporting team on serious violations against children to develop a joint plan of action to end the killing, assault, rape and other forms of sexual violence against children, while noting Myanmar's ratification of the Optional Protocol to the Convention on the Rights of the Child on the involvement of children in armed conflict;

(n) To cooperate and engage in a genuine dialogue with the new Special Rapporteur on the situation of human rights in Myanmar, the Independent Inquiry Mechanism and other United Nations mandate-holders and mechanisms dealing with the question of Myanmar, including by facilitating further visits and granting them unrestricted access throughout the country;

(o) Take concrete measures to strengthen institutional development and structural reform efforts, through a participatory and inclusive approach, to ensure respect for the rule of law, human rights and principles

Welcomes the recent one-year extension of the memorandum of understanding between the Government of Myanmar, the United Nations Development Programme and the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees to involve them in the implementation of bilateral arrangements with Bangladesh on the return of displaced persons from the Rakhine State, and stresses the importance of the Government of Myanmar continuing to cooperate fully with the Government of Bangladesh and the United Nations, in particular with the Office of the High Commissioner and in consultation with the populations concerned, in order to ensure the sustainable and voluntary return, in safety and dignity, to their places of origin in Myanmar, of all refugees and forcibly displaced persons, and to grant returnees freedom of movement and unhindered access to livelihoods, social services, including health services, education and housing and to compensate them for all losses suffered;

17. Calls for the early implementation of the memorandum of understanding signed by the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees and the United Nations Development Programme with the Government of Myanmar in 2018, and extended in 2019 and 2020, to help create conditions conducive to the return of refugees from Bangladesh;

18. Calls upon the international community to: (a) assist Bangladesh in providing humanitarian assistance to Rohingya refugees and forcibly displaced persons until they are voluntarily repatriated to Myanmar in safety and dignity; (b) assist Myanmar in providing humanitarian assistance to persons from all communities that have been displaced, including those in camps for internally displaced persons in the Rakhine State;

19. Urges the international community to support the joint response plan 2020 to the humanitarian crisis in the Rohingya region in order to ensure adequate capacity to respond to the humanitarian crisis;

20. Encourages all enterprises, including transnational corporations and national enterprises operating in Myanmar, to respect human rights in accordance with the Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights 17 and the recommendations of the independent international fact-finding mission on Myanmar contained in its report on the economic interests of the Myanmar armed forces;
21. Requests the Secretary-General:
(a) To continue to offer its good offices and to continue its discussions on Myanmar, involving all parties concerned, and to assist its Government;

(b) To extend the mission of its Special Envoy for Myanmar and to submit to the General Assembly at its seventy-sixth session the report of the Special Envoy on all relevant issues addressed in the present resolution;

(c) To provide all necessary assistance to its special envoy in order to enable it to carry out its mandate effectively and to provide an update to Member States, either every six months or when the situation on the ground so requires;
